answers.answer: Under Article 28(1) of the General Partnership Law 2004, each Partner is liable jointly and severally with the other Partners for all debts and obligations of the General Partnership incurred while he is a Partner, unless otherwise agreed by all the other Partners.

The maximum fine for failing to provide a copy of an assignment of rights to the Registered Agent or Registrar within 30 days is $1,500. This is based on Article 10(7) of the Foundations Law, which specifies a fine for a person failing to provide a copy of assignment of rights.

Under the Operating Law, the Board of Directors of the DIFCA is responsible for appointing and dismissing the Registrar. Specifically, the Board of Directors of the DIFCA shall appoint an individual to serve as the Registrar and may dismiss such a person from the office of Registrar. However, prior to appointing or dismissing the Registrar, the Board of Directors of the DIFCA must consult the President.

The result of the application heard in case CFI 057/2025 cannot be determined from the provided Context. The Context includes the case title and procedural background for CFI 057/2025, but it does not contain the final order or outcome of the specific application that was heard.

Based on the provided context, DIFC Law No. 2 of 2022 amended the following specific DIFC Laws: the Common Reporting Standard Law (DIFC Law No. 2 of 2018), the Employment Law (DIFC Law No. 2 of 2019), the General Partnership Law (DIFC Law No. 11 of 2004), the Law on the Application of Civil and Commercial Laws in the DIFC (DIFC Law No. 3 of 2004), the Personal Property Law (DIFC Law No. 9 of 2005), and the Limited Liability Partnership Law (DIFC Law No. 5 of 2004).
ONORA

The result of the application heard in case SCT 169/2025 was that the Defendant's application for an adjournment of the appeal hearing was refused. The hearing proceeded as scheduled on 15 December 2025 after the Defendant's initial request for an adjournment due to illness was denied, and a subsequent renewed application for adjournment by the Defendant's newly appointed legal representative was also refused.

The effective date for due diligence requirements for Pre-existing Accounts under the Common Reporting Standard Law 2018 is 31 December 2016, and for New Accounts it is 1 January 2017. This is based on Article 6 of the Law, which states these dates apply retrospectively.
According to Article 2 of the DIFC Personal Property Law 2005, this Law was made by the Ruler of Dubai.
Article 16(1)(c) of the Employment Law 2019 requires an Employer to keep records of 'the Employee's Remuneration (gross and net, where applicable), and the applicable Pay Period'.

The outcome of the specific order or application described in case CFI 016/2025 cannot be determined from the provided Context, as the Context does not contain any information referencing case CFI 016/2025.

The retention period for records under Article 12(4) of the Common Reporting Standard Law is six (6) years after the date of reporting the information. The provided context does not contain an Article 18(2)(b) of the General Partnership Law, so no retention period can be determined for that article.

The application in case CFI 067/2025 was dismissed. The Defendant's Application No. CFI-067-2025/1, which sought immediate judgment and/or strike out, was dismissed by H.E. Justice Shamlan Al Sawalehi in an order dated 26 January 2026. Consequently, the Applicant (Defendant) was ordered to pay the Respondent (Claimant) 80% of the Respondent's costs of the Application, assessed on the standard basis, totaling USD 155,879.50.

Under Article 12(5) of the Common Reporting Standard Law, a Reporting Financial Institution that obtains or creates records for any purpose under this Law in a language other than English must, upon request, provide an English translation to the Relevant Authority.

The date of enactment for the DIFC Personal Property Law 2005 is the date specified in the Enactment Notice in respect of this Law. The context does not provide the specific calendar date of enactment.

The context does not contain any information about a case with the reference SCT 514/2025, nor does it provide the last page of a document for that case. Therefore, the outcome of the specific order or application described in the question cannot be determined from the provided context.
The consolidated version of the Law on the Application of Civil and Commercial Laws in the DIFC was published in November 2024, as stated on the title page of the document.

The context does not contain the first page of a document for case CFI 010/2024, nor does it provide a summary of the court's final ruling for that specific case. The provided sources discuss procedural orders, legal interpretations, and rulings from other cases or hearings, but none are explicitly identified as the first page of the final ruling in CFI 010/2024.
Confirmation Statement
The outcome of the order in case SCT 295/2025 was that the Claimant's Application for Permission to Appeal the Judgment was refused, and there was no order as to costs.

According to the Conclusion section of case CA 009/2024, the Court of Appeal allowed the appeal in part. It restored the Second Part 50 order in its amended form against Ms Onita and dismissed the appeal against the Order insofar as it related to Mr Oron. The Court accepted the second ground of appeal, finding the Judge was wrong in his approach to and interpretation of the rule and in finding Ms Onita was not an Officer. Regarding costs, the Appellant succeeded substantially in establishing the principles and the Judge's error concerning Ms Onita but failed regarding the other target. Consequently, the Appellant is in principle entitled to its costs with a measure of discount, as there is a substantial disparity in the costs claimed by the parties.

Based on the context, a DIFC-incorporated General Partnership must preserve its accounting records for at least six (6) years from the date of their creation, as per Article 18(2)(b) of the General Partnership Law. Similarly, a DIFC-incorporated Limited Liability Partnership must preserve its accounting records for at least six (6) years from the date of their creation, as per Article 25(3)(b) of the Limited Liability Partnership Law. Both laws specify this as the minimum period, subject to any other period that may be prescribed in the Regulations.

The context does not contain any information about a jury decision in case ENF 269/2023. The provided document for ENF 269/2023 is an enforcement order from the Court of Appeal, and it does not mention a jury or a jury decision.

According to the 'IT IS HEREBY ORDERED THAT' section of arbitration case ARB 034/2025, the court decided three things. First, the interim anti-suit injunction (the ASI Order) was discharged with immediate effect. Second, the Defendant's application to set aside the ASI Order was granted. Third, the Claimant was ordered to pay the Defendant's costs of that set aside application on the standard basis.

According to the 'IT IS HEREBY ORDERED THAT' section of case SCT 454/2024, the application for permission to appeal was refused. Specifically, the order states: '1. The request for an oral hearing of the Application is refused. 2. The Application is refused.'
According to the 'IT IS HEREBY ORDERED THAT' section of case CFI 092/2024, the Defendants' application was dismissed. The claim is to proceed to trial to be determined alongside the main issues.
The DIFC Employment Law 2019 is administered by the DIFCA. This is explicitly stated in Article 8, which reads: 'This Law and any Regulations made under it shall be administered by the DIFCA.'
The Foundations Law is administered by the Registrar, as explicitly stated in Article 8 of the Foundations Law.
Article 9(1) of the Foundations Law grants the Board of Directors of the DIFCA the authority to make Regulations to facilitate the administration of, or further the objects of, this Law.
